learning_objectives: Trace the nociceptive pathway from peripheral stimulus to conscious pain perception, naming fiber types, neurotransmitters, and receptors at each relay
Explain wind-up and NMDA receptor activation at the dorsal horn and their clinical significance for chronic pain
Identify the descending inhibitory system including the PAG, descending tract, and primary neurotransmitters (serotonin and norepinephrine)
Name the two NMDA antagonist options taught in the fellowship with doses and appropriate clinical contexts
Distinguish peripheral sensitization from central sensitization clinically and pharmacologically
Recognize species-specific neuroanatomic differences relevant to analgesia in cats, rabbits, and other non-canine species
Explain cats' glucuronidation deficiency and its implications for analgesic drug selection and dosing
high_priority_objectives: Explain wind-up and NMDA receptor activation at the dorsal horn and their clinical significance for chronic pain
Distinguish peripheral sensitization from central sensitization clinically and pharmacologically
mastery_threshold_note: Fellows must connect neurophysiology to clinical drug selection. Naming mechanisms without applying them to treatment decisions indicates surface-level understanding.
